---
title: "Wolverine"
subtitle: |
  [NO_JERV_001]
indicatorID: 
  - NO_JERV_001
indicatorName: 
  - Wolverine [Jerv]
verbatimName: 
  - Jerv # the indicator name that us used in the native language
folderName: NO_JERV_001
country: Norway
continent: Europe
ECT: B1 - Compositional state characteristics
Realm: 
  - Terrestrial (T)
Biome: 
  - T6 Polar-alpine (cryogenic) biome
Ecosystem: 
  - NA
verbatimEcosystem: Fjell
yearAdded: 2026
yearLastUpdate: 2026
status: complete
Version: "1.0.0" # See wiki page: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/wiki#versioning
VersionComment: First version on ecRxiv, but this indicator is already published and described in the Norwegian Nature Index
url: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_JERV_001
Normalised: Yes
SpatialAggregationPathway: Aggregate (population model, rovdyrregion) - [Scale - Truncate] - Disaggregate (municipalities) - Aggregate (geographic region) - Aggregate (national level)
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
execute: 
  cache: false
author:
  - name: Anders Kolstad
    email: anders.kolstad@nina.no
    affiliations:
      - id: myID
        name: The Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA)
AuthorList: Kolstad, A.
date: June 29, 2026
data_availability: gold
code_reproducibility: gold
open_science_badge: gold
callout-icon: false
lightbox: true
code-links:
      - text: Add a review
        icon: github
        href: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv
hide: FALSE
bibliography: references.bib
csl: ambio.csl
codeReviewers:
  - name: Matthew Grainger
    email: matthew.grainger@nina.no
    affiliations:
      - id: reviewerInst
        name: Norwegian Institute for Nature Research
    reviewType: code review
    github: drmattg
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(here)
library(anybadger)
library(yaml)
library(tibble)
library(conflicted)
library(NIcalc)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(gt)
library(ecTools)
library(MetBrewer)
# remotes::install_github("NINAnor/ecTools") #version 0.0.0.9002
# This workflow uses `renv`. Please run `renv::restore()` in order to reproduce the working environment and run the analysis.
# renv::restore()
# NIcalc, and ecTools may need to be installed manually
```

```{r source}
#| echo: false
#| cache: false
source(here::here("_common.R"))
```

```{r reviewers}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

if (!is.null(yaml_data$codeReviewers)) {
  reviewers <- purrr::map_chr(yaml_data$codeReviewers, "name")
  
  htmltools::HTML(paste0(
    '<div id="title-block-header" class="quarto-title-block default">
       <div class="quarto-title-meta">
         <div>
           <div class="quarto-title-meta-heading">Reviewed by</div>
           <div class="quarto-title-meta-contents">',
    paste(reviewers, collapse = "; "),
    '</div>
         </div>
       </div>
     </div>',
    sep = ""
  ))
}
```

<!--# The following parts are autogenerated. Do not edit. -->

::: {layout-ncol="6"}

```{r statusBadge}
#| echo: false
status_badge(as.character(st))
```

```{r versionBadge}
#| echo: false
version_badge(my_version_number = as.character(version[[1]]))
```

```{r dataBadge}
#| echo: false
data_badge(as.character(badges[[1]]))
```

```{r codeBadge}
#| echo: false
code_badge(as.character(badges[[2]]))
```

```{r openScienceBadge}
#| echo: false
open_science_badge(as.character(badges[[3]]))
```

```{r licenseBadge}
#| echo: false
# The following inserts a badge specifying the GPL GNU version 3 license. ecRxiv recommends using this license, but you can decide to use another. Contact ecRxiv for help in insert a badge for a different license
license_badge()
```


::: 
 

> **Recommended citation**: `r paste(auth, " ", year, ". ", name, " (ID(s): ", folder_name, ") ", "v. ", version, ". ecRxiv: ", url, sep="")`



::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Metadata
```{r tbl-meta2}
#| tbl-cap: 'Indicator metadata'
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
meta |>
  dplyr::filter(Variable %in% c(
    "Indicator ID",
    "Indicator Name",
    "verbatimName",
    "Continent",
    "Country",
    "Ecosystem Condition Typology Class", 
    "Realm",
    "Biome",
    "Ecosystem",
    "verbatimEcosystem",
    "Year added", 
    "Last update",
    "Version",
    "Version comment",
    "Normalised",
    "Spatial aggregation pathway",
    "Precision")) |>   
  kable()

```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}

## Log

<!--# Update this logg with short messages for each update -->
- May 19 2026 - Started work
:::


<hr />

```{r fig-picture}
#| fig-cap: 'Wolverine. Photo: Musicaline. CC BY-SA 4-0.'
#| echo: false
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/Musicaline, CC BY-SA 4.0.png"))
```

<!--# Document you work below.  -->

## 1. Summary

<!--# 
With a maximum of 300 words, describe the indicator in general terms as you would to a non-expert. Think of this as a kind of common language summary. Follow the four numbers sub-headers below. under point 2, it is possible under  include a bullet point list of the specific steps in the workflow. 
 -->

1. **Background and rationale**:
<!-- What does the metric represent? Why is this relevant for describing ecosystem condition in this ecosystem?
What are the main anthropogenic impact factors? -->
Wolverine is a top predator in Norwegian mountains. It is mainly a scavenger, feeding on 
carcasses ranging from small rodents to reindeer. 
They require large territories in order to find enough food.
Historic over hunting is one reason for the low abundance we see today, but current management through culling is also a major determinant.  

2. **Methods**:
<!-- What kind of data is used? How is the data customized (modified, estimated, integrated) to fit its purpose as an indicator? -->
Wolverine numbers are reported each year by *Statens Naturoppsyn* (SNO) for each of the eight carnivore management regions in Norway. These estimates are associated with relativey small uncertainties. 
We get the data from the Norwegian Nature Index database [@nater_naturindeks_2025] where also the X~100~ reference values are stored.
These represent the hypothetical number of wolverine in Norwegian alpine ecosystems given minimal anthropogenic influence, and are based on expert opinion. 


3. **Interpretation**:
<!-- Add a very short description of how the indicator values should be interpreted. Here's an example for a bumblebee indicator: An indicator value of 1 signifies an intact bumblebee community. Values decreasing toward zero reflect negative ecological change, specifically the decline of species expected to be present. This metric does not account for potentially positive changes, such as increases in species' abundance or the arrival of new species. Tip: Add text on the line directly following the subheader, and don't introduce blank lines. This way the whole paragraph renders as a block quote. -->
An indicator value of 1 means that wolverine are as numerous, or more, than what we expect under the reference condition.
Values decreasing toward zero indicates that the average population size is decreasing for each carnivore area.

4. **Results and development needs**:
<!-- Briefly, what does the indicator say about the ecosystem condition. What is the current status of  the metric (can it be used or is it still in development), and what immediate tasks remain to improve the indicator or to make the indicator operational -->
The indicator value for Norway as a whole in 2024 is 0.14 [95% CI = 0.13-0.14]. This is extremely low and reflects the both the historical overexploitation and current management. The indicator value is highest in eastern Norway, and lowest in the south and western Norway, where it is more or less abscent. Development needs include a revision of the X~100~ values based on updated knowledge, and a more structured approach to expert elicitation (or other methods).     

## 1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

1. **Bakgrunn og begrunnelse**: 
Jerv er en topp-predator i norske fjellområder. Den er hovedsakelig åtseleter og lever av kadavre i størrelsen fra smågnagere til rein.
Jerv trenger store leveområder for å finne nok mat.
Historisk overbeskatning er en viktig årsak til den lave bestanden vi ser i dag, men dagens forvaltning gjennom lisensfelling er også en sentral faktor.

2. **Metoder**: 
Jervebestanden rapporteres hvert år av Statens naturoppsyn (SNO) for hver av de åtte rovviltregionene, og disse estimatene er knyttet til relativt små usikkerheter.
Dataene som brukes i denne indikatoren hentes fra Naturindeks-databasen [@nater_naturindeks_2025], hvor også referanseverdiene er lagret.
Referanseverdiene representerer det hypotetiske antallet jerv i norske alpine økosystemer under minimal menneskelig påvirkning, og er i stor grad basert på ekspertvurderinger.

3. **Tolkning**: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at jervebestanden er like stor eller større enn det vi forventer under referansetilstanden. Verdier som nærmer seg null indikerer at den gjennomsnittlige bestandsstørrelsen på rovviltregionsnivå er avtagende og går mot null.

4. **Resultater og utviklingsbehov**: 
Indikatorverdien for Norge som helhet i 2024 er 0,14 [95 % KI = 0,13–0,14]. Dette er svært lavt og reflekterer både historisk jakt og dagens forvaltning. Indikatorverdien er høyest i Øst-Norge og lavest i Sør- og Vest-Norge, hvor jerv er mer eller mindre fraværende. Utviklingsbehov inkluderer en revisjon av X~100~-verdiene basert på oppdatert kunnskap, samt en mer strukturert tilnærming til ekspertvurderinger (eller andre metoder).

## 2. About the underlying data

<!--# Give a general introduction the datasets you have used, and provide more specific details in the sub-chapters.  -->
The data comes from the national carnivore monitoring program (www.rovdata.no). Variable estimates are based on @milleret2024estimates for 2024, and @bischof2019rovquant for 2019 and 2014. Estimated for earlier years are partlybased on expert opinion (see [the Norwegian Natur index websites](https://www.naturindeks.no/Indicators/jerv))

### 2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

<!--# Describe the temporal and spatial resolution and extent of the data used -->
Indicator values are available for the different assessment years in  _Naturindeksen_: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019 and 2024. 

The indicator is calculated for eight carnivore regions in Norway. These may overlap with one or more of the geographic regions that we use here. Here we report the values for five geographic regions, and for Norway as a whole. 

### 2.2 Sampling design

<!-- How was the data sampled? Describe potential sampling biases -->

The data is collected by 'Statens Naturoppsyn' (SNO) by different means. including DNA sampling from feces, hair and urine. The sampling is not systematic, but more or less complete. 

### 2.3 Original units

<!--# What are the original units for the most relevant  variables in the data-->
The original units of the raw data is the number of individuals. The data are assigned to spatial units, so this could be turned into measures of density.

### 2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

<!--# Is the data openly available? If not, how can one access it? What are the key references to the datasets?   -->
The underlying data are available via rovdata.no. Indicator values from the Norwegian Nature Index can be found in [@nater_ni2025_2025].
Indicator values, variable values and reference values can be downloaded from the Nature Index database using the API, as I have done below.
The data is licenced under [CC-BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).



<!-- ### 2.5 Additional comments about the dataset -->

<!--# Optional. Text here -->

## 3. Indicator properties

### 3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

<!--# Describe the rationale for assigning the indicator to the ECT class. See https://oneecosystem.pensoft.net/article/58218/
This doesnt need to be very long. Maybe just a single sentence. -->
B1 - Compositional state characteristics

<!-- 
### 3.1.1 Other condition typologies -->

<!--# Optional: Add text about other standards or typologies, such as alternative national condition typologies, and how the indicator relates to these -->


### 3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

<!--# 

Describe the ecosystem condition characteristic represented in the indicator. See 10.3897/oneeco.6.e58218 for information on what these characteristics might be.
For example, and indicator called 'Trenching in mires' could be made to represent an ecosystem characteristic 'Intact hydrology'. The term 'characteristic' is used similar to the term 'criteria' in Multiple Criteria Decision Making.  

-->

The ecosystem characteristic represented in this indicator is an ecosystem where species abundances and composition is similar to that in the reference condition. 

### 3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

<!--# Describe known collinearities with other metrics (indicators or variables) that could become problematic if they were also included in the same Ecosystem Condition Assessment as the indicator described here. -->

None that are very strong.

### 3.4 Impact factors

<!--# Describe the main natural and anthropogenic factors that affecst the metric -->
Wolverine still suffers from very strong hunting from year 1700 up until it was protected in 1973. 
The population today is also heavily regulated by culling. 
Wolverine is also affected by other human activities such as land use change or disturbance.


### 3.5 Spatial aggregation

<!-- Describe in word the same aggregation pathway presented in the top yaml (the metadata). If you have scaled against the reference value, it is especially important to know if you did any avergaing or summations etc. beforehand. An example text for a rather simple case could be: "The variable is scaled at the initial polygon level and the point estimate is a area-weighted arithmetic mean of all polygins. There is no transformation, and truncation is not needed as the indicator becomes naturally bound between 0 and 1".  -->

A model is used to estimate an aggregated wolverine count at the scale of carnivore regions. Here the variable is normalised by scaling and truncating against X~100~ and X~0~. The indicator values are downscaled to the level of municipalities before they are aggregated to the five geographic regions, and then to Norway, with a sample weight proportional to the mountain area in each municipality or region. 


## 4. Reference condition and levels

### 4.1 Reference condition

<!--# Define the reference condition (or refer to where it is defined). Note the distinction between reference condition and reference levels 10.3897/oneeco.5.e58216  -->
The reference condition is a mountain ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the period 1961-1990 [@nybo_fagsystem_2017].

### 4.2 Reference levels

<!--# 

If relevant (i.e. if you have normalised a variable), describe the reference levels used to normalise the variable. 

Use the terminology where X~0~ refers to the reference level (the variable value) denoting the worst possible condition; X~100~denotes the optimum or best possible condition; and X~*n*~, where in is between 0 and 100, denotes any other anchoring points linking the variable scale to the indicator scale (e.g. the threshold value between good and bad condition X~60^). 

Why was the current option chosen and how were the reference levels quantified? If the reference values are calculated as part of the analyses further down, please repeat the main information here.

 -->

X~100~ is defined based on the predicted number of wolverine individuals based on a statistical model [@bischof2019rovquant; @milleret2024estimates]. This value varies between carnivore regions.

X~0~ is defined as a complete absence of wolverine.

<!-- X~60~ is not specified and defaults to 60% of the X~100~ value.  -->

#### 4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

<!--# 

Describe the spatial resolution of the reference levels. E.g. is it defined as a fixed value for all areas, or does it vary. Also, at what spatial scale are the reference levels valid? For example, if the reference levels have a regional resolution (varies between regions), it might mean that it is only valid and correct to use for normalising local variable values that are first aggregated to regional scale. However, sometimes the reference levels are insensitive to this and can be used to scale variables at the local (e.g. plot) scale. 

 -->
 The reference values vary between the eight carnivore management regions, and normalisation of variable values is therefore done at this scale as well. 


## 5. Uncertainties
### 5.1 General uncertainties
<!--# Describe the main reasons why you might not trust 100% the final value to represent the given ecosystem characteristic for the give ecosystem type. What aspects of this condition metric are most sensitive to subjective decitions? Is the spatial sampling sufficient? Does the choice of modeling framework affect the values? Is the metric relevant do describe ecosystem condition for the give ecosystem type?  -->
One main source of uncertainty is about how the X~100~ is set using expert opinion, based on assumptions about the amount of suitable habitat and the density of reproducible units(individuals).
There is also some uncertainty about the need to define an X~60~ value. This will, however, have little influence on the indicators values, since they are going to be much closer to the X~0~value in any case.


### 5.2 Reported uncertainties
<!-- Describe the nature of the errors you (hopefully) provide alongside the point estimate for the condition metric. It is especially important to clarify if the errors are inferential (referring to the uncertainty of the point estimate) or descriptive (referreing to the data itself). Here' an example text: "What we report as uncertainties in @tbl-finalTable are inferential and refer to the uncertainty around the point estimates. It does not directly describe variation in the data itself. 
However, the variation in the mean is defined by the spatial variation only, and quartiles are obtained by bootstrapping the set of localities within a region, and calculating the indicator for each of 9999 such samples".   -->

The reported uncertainties reflect the uncertainties around the true parameter means, i.e. they are inferrential uncertainties for the true value of the indicator for the given area and time combination.  

## 6. References

<!--# You can add references manually or use a citation manager and add intext citations as with crossreferencing and hyperlinks. See https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/footnotes-and-citations.html -->

::: {#refs}
:::

## 7. Import and prepare datasets

<!--# All the data import and preparation should be done in this chapter, before the analysis chapter. As a minimum, add code for importing data, and describe or show the data structure. You may also explain certain variables, what they represent, and their distributions -->

### 7.1 Naturindeks

<!--# Import and prepare the first dataset for analysis. Change the header from Dataset A to the name of the actual dataset. -->

We download the dataset from the Naturindeks database using the API. 
The function gives read-only access without requiring a username and password.
```{r}
#| eval: false
# I use a dev version of NIcalc where the username and passwork for the guest user is hrd coded into the function. This will probably be merged into the real NIcalc (from NINAnor/NIcalc) soon.
# renv::install("ChloeRN/NIcalc@include_ReadOnly_access")
# library(NIcalc)

# Import data set
jervImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Jerv"),
  year = c("2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)
saveRDS(jervImport, here::here("data/jervImport.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| include: false
jervImport <- readRDS(here::here("data/jervImport.rds"))
```



## 8. Spatial units

<!--# Describe the spatial units that you rely on in your analyses. Highlight the spatial units (the resolution) that the indicator values should be interpreted at. Potential spatial delineation data (maksing layers etc.) should be introduced under chapert 7. We recommend using the SEEA EA terminology of Basic Spatial Units (BSU), Ecosystem Asses (EA) and Ecosystem Accounting Area (EAA). -->

According to the Naturindeks terminology, the 430 municipalities are called `BSunits`. 
The Carnivore regions (Rovviltregion) are subsamples of `BSunits`, and they are referred to as `ICunits`.
The five geographic regions in Norway (east, south, west, central, north) are called `parts` and `NIunits`. 
We can think of the carnivore regions as ecosystem assets, and mainland Norway as the Ecosystem accounting Area.

```{r fig-carnivoreRegions}
#| fig-cap: 'Map of the eight carnivore management regions in Norway.'
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/rovviltregioner.png"))
```

## 9. Analyses

Here I use a generic approach to handling data from the Naturindeks where I get both the variable values and the reference values, separately, from the NI database, and perform the normalisation and spatial aggregation ourselves.

The reference values have uncertainties attached to them, which we will ignore here.
The variable values have uncertainties as well, in the form of probability function. 
The uncertainties are from a statistical model, and so we think of them as inferential uncertainties. 

Here I use an NIcalc function to just prepare and structure the data a little differently.  
```{r assemble}
#| message: false
# Assemble
assemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = jervImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)
```


Then I get the reference values. 
These are unique to each carnivore region. 
I only have an X~100~ value. X~0~ is a natural zero.

```{r refs}
x100 <- jervImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
  select(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue)
gt::gt(x100)
```


The variable values are stored as a parameterised probability function, and I need to draw *n* samples from this function. 
The probability function differs between indicators in the Naturindeks.
It could also differ within an indicator.
Here I create a vector defining which probability function to use. 
I use ´tradObs´ where there is a regular probability distribution family, and `customObs` where  customDist!= NA. When `distributionFamilyName == NA`, `customDistribution != NA`.

```{r vars} 
myYears <- as.character(c(2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  obs <- assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName

  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"

  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
```

Now I am ready to sample the probability distributions. 
I set *n* to be 1000.
```{r draw}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
dat <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in 1:length(myYears)) {
  #print(i)
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = assemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  
  myMat <-  myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId")) |>
    select(-name) |>
    add_column(year = myYears[i])
  
  dat <- dat |> 
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(dat)
```

This is the point in the spatial aggregation where we should normalise against the reference values.
So let's add in the X~100~ values.

```{r}
dat <- dat |>
  left_join(x100 |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)), by = join_by(ICunitId))
```

Then we can normalise also the scatter plot with variables against indicator values (@fig-norm).

```{r}
ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = dat$value,
  x100 = dat$x100
)

dat <- dat |> add_column(indicator = ind)
```

```{r normplot}
#| eval: true
temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(.~dat$ICunitName) +
  labs(x = "Variable values (number of individuals)")
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng.png"), scale =1.5, plot =temp)
```

```{r fig-norm}
#| fig-cap: "Normalisation (scaling and truncation) of variable values into indicator values. The X100 value differs between 15 counties in Norway."
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/norm_plot_eng.png"))
```

I also export a figure for the Norwegian fact sheet:

```{r expPlot}
#| code-summary: 'Export normalisation function plots'
#| eval: false
temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk") +
  labs(x = "Antall jerv", y = "Idndikatorverdi") +
  facet_wrap(.~dat$ICunitName)
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot_facet.png"), scale =1.5, plot =temp)

temp <- ec_norm_plot(dat$value, dat$indicator, lan = "norsk") +
  labs(x = "Antall jerv", y = "Indikatorverdi")
ggsave(here::here("img/norm_plot.png"), scale =1, plot =temp)
```

Then we estimate values for each geographic region (N, M, E, S, W).
In *Naturindeks* this is done behind the scenes, and I'm not sure how.
I will downscale the indicator values from carnivore regions to BSunits, and then, based on the alpine area of each BSunit, I will upscale from BSunits to NIunits. 


```{r}
# The link between BSunits and NIunits can be found here:
bs_ni <- as.data.frame(assemble$NIunits) |>
  rownames_to_column(var = "BSunits")
head(bs_ni)
# And the link between BSunits and ICunits can be found here:
bs_ic <- as.data.frame(assemble$ICunits) |>
  rownames_to_column(var = "BSunits") |>
  rename(ICunits = Jerv)
head(bs_ic)
# and then I combine them
bs_ni_ic <- bs_ni |>
  left_join(bs_ic, by = join_by(BSunits))
head(bs_ni_ic)
# and we also get the alpine area
bs_fjell <- as.data.frame(assemble$BSunits) |>
  select(BSunits = name, Fjell) |>
  mutate(BSunits = as.character(BSunits))
head(bs_fjell)
# and add that as well
bs_ni_ic2 <- bs_ni_ic |>
  left_join(bs_fjell, by = join_by(BSunits)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunits))
head(bs_ni_ic2)
tail(bs_ni_ic2)
# and add the new columns to the main dataset
head(dat)
dat_expand <- dat |>
  right_join(
    bs_ni_ic2,
    by = join_by(ICunitId),
    unmatched = "error",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  )
head(dat_expand)
# and we create a column for the regions
dat_expand2 <- dat_expand |>
  select(-wholeArea) |>
  mutate(across(c(E, S, W, C, N), ~ na_if(., 0))) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(E, S, W, C, N), values_drop_na = T, values_to = "count", names_to = "part") |>
  select(-count)
head(dat_expand2)
```



### Spatial aggregation

Then we can aggregate from municipalities to geographic regions using `ecTools`.
This uses a Monte Carlo propagation of the uncertainties. 

This takes a while to run with n = 1000.

```{r aggregate1}
#| cache: true
#| eval: false
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = dat_expand2,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = Fjell,
  start_units = BSunits,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/mc_propagated.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| include: false
mc_propagated <- readRDS(here::here("data/mc_propagated.rds"))
```


To get the national indicator estimate, we will do another MC propagation from geographic regions to national scale. 
So we need the summed mountain area per geographic region.

```{r}
aux <- dat_expand2 |>
  select(-indicator) |>
  distinct(BSunits, .keep_all = T) |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(alpine_area = sum(Fjell)) 
aux
```

```{r aggToNorway}
#| cache: true
#| eval: false
mc_propagated <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(aux, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated2 <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = alpine_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated2, here::here("data/mc_propagated2.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| include: false
mc_propagated2 <- readRDS(here::here("data/mc_propagated2.rds"))
```

Then we append this to the main dataset

```{r}
mc_propagated3 <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated2)
```

## 10. Results


Then we create some summary statistics (@tbl-results) and plot them (@fig-timeSeries).

```{r summaryStats}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
summary_out <- mc_propagated3 |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975), 2),
  ) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B1",
    Indicator = "Wolverine",
    Ecosystem = "Mountains",
    ID = "NO_JERV_001"
  )
```

```{r prepping}
part_labels <- c(
  N = "North",
  C = "Central",
  W = "West",
  S = "South",
  E = "East",
  Norway = "Norway",
  All = "Norway"
)

region_levels <- c("Norway", "Central", "East", "North", "South", "West")

region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(region_levels)),
  region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = myYears),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region"),
    part2 = recode(part, !!!part_labels)
  )
```
```{r fig-timeSeries}
#| fig-cap: 'Indicator values for NO_PTAR_001 (ptarmigan) plotted over time and for five different regions, as well as the combined national estimated. The error bars are 95% confidence intervals.'
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part2, group = part2)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 2, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(
      nrow = 2,
      override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)
    ),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
```



```{r tbl-results}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| tbl-cap: 'Indicator values and uncertainty estimates for NO_PTAR_001.'

summary_out |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  knitr::kable()
```


## 11. Export file

The indicator values are exported and can be found under `data/results_NO_PTAR_001_v1.0.0.csv`.

```{r export}
#| eval: false
#| code-summary: 'Export data'
write.csv(mc_propagated3, here::here("data/results_NO_JERV_001_v1.0.0.csv"), row.names = F)
```

## 12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on _Naturindeks_ [@nater_naturindeks_2025].



::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Session Info

This workflow uses `renv`. Run `renv::restore()` to recreate the working environment. 
 
:::
